g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of interrupt control. We will learn why and how to enable and disable interrupts in computer systems.
g.text: Understanding Interrupt Control in Computer Systems

g.data-narration: An interrupt is a signal that suspends the current program execution to handle urgent events requiring immediate CPU attention.
g.text: 1. Suspend execution → 2. Save state → 3. Execute ISR → 4. Restore state → 5. Resume

g.data-narration: Interrupts are classified as hardware-generated, software-generated, and as maskable or non-maskable based on CPU control.
The Interrupt Flag (IF) in the processor status register controls whether the CPU recognizes maskable interrupts.
Different processor architectures provide unique instructions to control interrupt states, from x86 CLI/STI to ARM CPSID.
Critical sections require atomic execution without interruption to maintain data consistency across main code and ISRs.
Without interrupt control, a race condition occurs when an ISR modifies shared variables mid-operation, causing data inconsistency.
The solution wraps critical operations with DI (disable interrupts) and EI (enable interrupts) instructions to ensure atomicity.
g.text: Interrupts disabled during critical section only

g.data-narration: The standard critical section pattern saves state, disables interrupts, performs operations, then restores previous interrupt state.
Disabling interrupts risks missing time-critical events, system unresponsiveness, and watchdog timer resets if held too long.
Keep critical sections short, save and restore state, use fine-grained locking, and measure maximum disable duration.
Modern processors support interrupt priority levels, allowing finer control than simple enable/disable with nested interrupt handling.
Nested interrupts occur when higher priority interrupts are serviced while lower priority ISR is still executing after re-enabling.
g.text: Higher priority interrupts can preempt lower priority ISR after EI instruction within ISR

g.data-narration: A producer-consumer buffer uses interrupt disabling to protect shared read/write indices from race conditions.
To summarize: interrupts manage asynchronous events, critical sections require atomic execution, and keeping sections short ensures system responsiveness.
g.text: 1. Interrupts signal urgent events requiring CPU attention
2. IF flag controls maskable interrupt recognition
4. Keep critical sections short for responsiveness
